docs.title: VPNConnect Documentation
docs.text:
This documentation follows the Diátaxis framework, organised into four distinct sections based on what you need:

Section What it is Start here when… Tutorials Guided learning experiences You are new to VPNConnect How-to guides Goal-oriented step-by-step directions You need to accomplish a specific task Reference Technical descriptions and facts You need to look something up Explanation Background, concepts, and design decisions You want to understand why

docs.title: Tutorials
docs.text:
Learning-oriented guides that take you through a meaningful task from start to finish.

  • Getting started: your first VPN connection

docs.title: How-to guides
docs.text:
Task-oriented directions for accomplishing specific goals.

Installation & setup - How to install VPNConnect - How to uninstall VPNConnect - How to build from source

Day-to-day usage - How to manage VPN profiles - How to connect with MFA (Microsoft SSO) - How to connect in standalone mode (no daemon)

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
Technical descriptions of the CLI, protocol, and configuration.

  • CLI reference
  • Pulse protocol attributes

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text:
Background reading that explains concepts, design decisions, and how things work.

  • Architecture: daemon, IPC, and connection lifecycle
  • Pulse protocol internals
  • MFA browser automation
  • PAC file parsing
  • Logout and session termination

docs.title: Downloads
docs.text: Latest release: github.com/vpnconnectdev/vpnconnectdev.github.io/releases/latest

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: Package Format Notes vpnconnect-linux-amd64 Raw binary No install, run directly vpnconnect_*.deb Debian/Ubuntu sudo dpkg -i vpnconnect-*.rpm Red Hat/Fedora/SUSE sudo rpm -i vpnconnect-*.pkg.tar.zst Arch Linux sudo pacman -U vpnconnect-*.apk Alpine sudo apk add --allow-untrusted vpnconnect-*.ipk OpenWrt opkg install

docs.title: macOS
docs.text: Package Format Notes vpnconnect-darwin-universal Universal binary (arm64 + amd64) Runs natively on Apple Silicon and Intel vpnconnect-darwin-arm64 arm64 binary Apple Silicon vpnconnect-darwin-amd64 amd64 binary Intel *-macos-installer.pkg macOS installer Recommended — installs daemon + CLI vpnconnect-macos.app.zip App bundle Drag to Applications

docs.title: Windows
docs.text: Package Format Notes vpnconnect-windows-installer.exe NSIS installer Recommended vpnconnect-windows-amd64.exe Raw binary No install

docs.title: Verify checksums
docs.text:
Each release includes a checksums.txt. Verify with:

sha256sum --check checksums.txt

docs.title: Architecture: daemon, IPC, and connection lifecycle
docs.text: This document explains the overall design of VPNConnect: how the daemon, CLI, and UI relate to each other, how they communicate, and how a connection moves through its lifecycle.

docs.title: The daemon/server model
docs.text:
VPNConnect uses a privileged daemon pattern. A long-running server process owns the VPN interface with root privileges, while lightweight client processes (CLI commands, the UI) communicate with it over a local TCP socket.

 ┌──────────────────────┐     JSON over TCP      ┌──────────────────────────┐
 │  vpnconnect connect  │ ──────────────────────► │  vpnconnect server       │
 │  vpnconnect status   │ ◄────────────────────── │  (daemon, runs as root)  │
 │  vpnconnect ui       │                         │  owns TUN/TAP interface  │
 └──────────────────────┘                         └──────────────────────────┘
        clients                                         server / daemon

Why this split?

Creating and managing TUN/TAP interfaces requires elevated privileges on all supported platforms. The daemon runs once as root (or SYSTEM on Windows) and stays running; unprivileged users interact with the VPN without ever requiring sudo themselves.

docs.title: Server info file
docs.text:
When the daemon starts it binds to a random local TCP port and writes a small JSON file that clients read to locate it:

Platform Path Linux /run/vpnconnect/server.json macOS /var/run/vpnconnect/server.json Windows %ProgramData%\vpnconnect\server.json

The file contains the port number and a random auth token generated at startup. Every client request must include this token in the message headers.

docs.title: IPC protocol
docs.text:
Communication is a line-delimited JSON request/response protocol sent over the local TCP connection.

Request message

{
  "command": "connect",
  "headers": { "token": "<auth-token>" },
  "body": { "params": "<serialized-session-json>" }
}

Response message

{
  "status": "ok",
  "headers": {},
  "body": { "result": "connected" }
}

A status of "error" means the body["error"] field contains an error string.

Supported commands: connect, disconnect, status, statistics.

docs.title: Package map
docs.text:
internal/
├── pulse/           Pulse protocol implementation
│   ├── auth/        IF-T/TLS + EAP authentication
│   ├── esp/         ESP/DTLS data tunnel, key derivation
│   ├── tls/         TLS-only data channel handler
│   ├── config/      Network config parsing (AVP attributes)
│   ├── avp/         Attribute-Value Pair encoding/decoding
│   ├── ift/         IF-T packet framing
│   ├── eap/         EAP message types
│   └── pac/         PAC file extraction for DNS domains
├── tuntap/          TUN/TAP interface management (platform abstraction)
├── platformnet/     OS-specific network operations (routes, DNS, interfaces)
├── mfa/             Browser automation for Microsoft SSO/Azure AD
├── profile/         VPN profile storage with OS keychain integration
├── server/          Daemon process (TCP server, command dispatcher)
├── session/         Connection parameter serialisation for IPC
├── protocol/        JSON message framing shared by server and clients
├── icons/           Embedded application icons
└── log/             Structured logger

docs.title: Connection lifecycle
docs.text:
A full connection passes through these stages in order. The order is critical: certain steps must happen before others to avoid routing loops and kernel ordering constraints.

connect()
│
├─ 1. Authenticate
│      IF-T/TLS handshake → EAP flow → receive session token / DSID
│
├─ 2. Receive configuration
│      Parse AVP attributes: IP, netmask, MTU, DNS, routes, PAC
│
├─ 3. Determine ESP server IP  (if ESP is enabled)
│
├─ 4. ESP handshake  ◄── must happen BEFORE TUN creation
│      Negotiate keys, set up UDP ESP tunnel
│
├─ 5. Add route exception for VPN server IP
│      Pins the server to the default gateway so traffic to it
│      is NOT routed back into the VPN tunnel
│
├─ 6. Create TUN/TAP interface
│
├─ 7. Configure interface
│      Assign IP/mask, set MTU, add routes, configure DNS
│
└─ 8. Start data channel handler
       ESP handler (UDP) or TLS handler (TCP fallback)

Disconnection reverses these steps: the data channel is stopped, routes are removed, the TUN/TAP interface is torn down, and a logout packet is sent to the server (see Logout and session termination).

docs.title: Platform abstraction
docs.text:
Platform-specific code lives in separate files selected at build time using Go build tags:

Area Linux macOS Windows TUN/TAP tuntap/platform_linux.go tuntap/platform_darwin.go tuntap/platform_windows.go Routes/DNS/interfaces platformnet/platformnet_linux.go platformnet/platformnet_darwin.go platformnet/platformnet_windows.go Interface naming tun0, tun1, … utun0, utun1, … wintun DNS configuration systemd-resolved DNSZones winipcfg (wireguard/windows) Wintun driver — — wintun.dll

The tuntap.Manager interface and the platformnet package provide a unified API that the rest of the codebase uses; platform differences are hidden behind those abstractions.

docs.title: Standalone mode
docs.text:
When vpnconnect connect --standalone is used, no daemon is involved. The CLI process itself creates the TUN interface and manages the data channel, blocking until interrupted. This mode:

  • Requires the process to have root/administrator privileges directly.
  • Is useful on systems where installing the daemon is not practical.
  • Does not support status or statistics queries from a separate terminal (because there is no daemon to query).

See How to connect in standalone mode for usage details.

docs.title: Logout and session termination
docs.text: This document explains how VPNConnect terminates a VPN session both on the client side and on the server side, and why using a proper logout sequence matters.

docs.title: Why a proper logout matters
docs.text:
Simply closing the TLS connection (or killing the process) leaves the session alive on the Pulse Secure server. This causes two problems:

  1. Resource waste: the server holds onto the session slot, firewall rules, and allocated IP address until its own idle-timeout expires.
  2. Session-limit errors: many Pulse Secure deployments impose a limit on concurrent sessions per user. An orphaned session counts against this limit. On the next connection attempt the user may see a "maximum sessions reached" dialog and need to manually evict the old session (this is also what the MFA browser's session-limit handling covers — see MFA browser automation).

Sending an explicit logout packet mirrors the behaviour of the official Pulse Secure client and of OpenConnect, on whose implementation vpnconnect's logout is modelled.

docs.title: Dual-method logout
docs.text:
vpnconnect attempts logout using two complementary methods in sequence:

Disconnect() called
       │
       ▼
logoutIFT()  —  send IF-T/TLS packet (vendor=Juniper, type=0x89)
       │
       │  if the TLS connection is already closed or the packet fails
       ▼
logoutHTTP()  —  GET https://<server>/dana-na/auth/logout.cgi
       │
       ▼
Close TLS connection, stop data channel, remove routes, destroy TUN interface

Errors from either logout step are logged but do not prevent the rest of the disconnect sequence from completing.

docs.title: IF-T/TLS logout packet
docs.text:
The primary method sends a Juniper-specific IF-T/TLS control packet:

Vendor:   0x0a4c  (Juniper Networks)
Type:     0x89    (Logout / IFTClientLogout)
Length:   16      (header only, no payload)
Sequence: current IF-T sequence number
Payload:  (empty)

This constant (IFTClientLogout = 0x89) is defined in internal/pulse/ift/packet.go and matches the value used by OpenConnect.

docs.title: HTTP logout fallback
docs.text:
If the IF-T/TLS packet cannot be sent (e.g. the TLS connection was already closed by the server), vpnconnect falls back to an HTTP GET request:

GET /dana-na/auth/logout.cgi HTTP/1.1
Host: <vpn-server>
Cookie: DSID=<session-cookie>

This endpoint is standard across Pulse Secure / Ivanti Connect Secure installations and also mirrors OpenConnect's oncp_bye() function. It works even after the IF-T tunnel has been torn down, as it is a plain HTTPS request.

docs.title: Sequence number tracking
docs.text: To construct valid IF-T packets after authentication, vpnconnect tracks a uint32 sequence counter (iftSeq) inside the Client struct. This counter is incremented for each IF-T packet sent, including the logout packet, ensuring the server accepts it as a legitimate continuation of the session.

docs.title: MFA browser automation
docs.text: This document explains how VPNConnect handles Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for enterprise VPN endpoints that use Microsoft Azure AD / Office 365 sign-in.

docs.title: Why browser automation?
docs.text:
Pulse Secure VPN endpoints integrated with Microsoft SSO do not expose a machine-readable API for the authentication step. The only way to authenticate is through the browser-based sign-in flow. VPNConnect opens a real Chrome browser (via the chromedp library) and, optionally, automates the credential-entry steps so the user only needs to approve a push notification or enter a TOTP code.

The browser opens visibly so the user can complete the MFA prompt (authenticator app push, SMS code, etc.). Once the authentication succeeds, vpnconnect extracts the DSID session cookie from the browser and closes the window.

docs.title: Automation modes
docs.text:
VPNConnect supports two modes for the browser-based authentication flow.

Manual mode (default)

The browser navigates to the VPN endpoint URL and waits. The user fills in all credentials and completes any MFA challenge themselves. This works with any identity provider — Microsoft SSO, other SAML/OIDC providers, or custom login pages.

Automated mode (--mfa-automate)

The browser navigates to the VPN endpoint and automatically fills in the Microsoft SSO username and password fields. The user only needs to complete the MFA challenge (push notification, TOTP code, etc.). This mode is tailored to the Microsoft Azure AD / Office 365 login page selectors and may break if Microsoft changes the page layout.

Enable it permanently in the profile:

vpnconnect profile add --name work --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice@example.com --mfa --mfa-automate

Or override for a single invocation:

vpnconnect connect --profile work --mfa-automate
vpnconnect mfa --profile work --mfa-automate

docs.title: The DSID cookie
docs.text:
The DSID cookie is the Pulse Secure session token. Once obtained, it is passed directly to the VPN authentication step in place of a username/password, bypassing the MFA challenge for the duration of the session.

When the connect command detects that a profile has MFAEnabled: true and no cached DSID is available, the MFA flow is triggered automatically before the connection request is sent to the daemon.

The mfa command can be used to pre-obtain the DSID cookie without immediately connecting:

vpnconnect mfa --profile work

docs.title: Authentication flow
docs.text:
1.  Open Chrome (non-headless, so user sees the window)
2.  Navigate to the VPN endpoint URL
3.  Handle pre-auth proceed button (if present)          →  #sn-preauth-proceed_2
4.  [auto only] Fill in username                         →  //input[@name="loginfmt"]
5.  [auto only] Click submit                             →  //input[@type="submit"]
6.  [auto only] Fill in password                         →  #passwordInput or input[name="passwd"]
7.  [auto only] Click Microsoft sign-in button           →  *[id*=idSIButton9]
8.  User completes MFA (push / TOTP / SMS)
9.  Handle post-auth proceed button (if present)         →  #sn-postauth-proceed_2
10. Handle session-limit dialog (if present)             →  #postfixSID_1, #btnContinue
11. Extract DSID cookie
12. Close the browser and return DSID to caller

Steps 3, 9, and 10 are conditional — not all deployments show these UI elements. Steps 4–7 only run when --mfa-automate is enabled; in manual mode the user fills in credentials themselves.

docs.title: Selectors reference
docs.text: Element Selector Notes Pre-auth proceed button #sn-preauth-proceed_2 VPN-specific; optional Username field //input[@name="loginfmt"] Microsoft SSO standard Submit button //input[@type="submit"] Password field #passwordInput[name="Password"] Microsoft SSO variant 1 Password field (alt) input[name="passwd"] Microsoft SSO variant 2 Sign-in button *[id*=idSIButton9] Microsoft SSO standard Post-auth proceed button #sn-postauth-proceed_2 VPN-specific; optional Session-limit radio #postfixSID_1 Shown when max sessions reached Continue button #btnContinue Session-limit confirmation

docs.title: Timeout
docs.text: The MFA handler waits up to 5 minutes for the user to complete the MFA challenge. If the DSID cookie is not obtained within that window, the operation fails and an error is returned.

docs.title: When MFA is not needed
docs.text:
If a profile has MFAEnabled: false, the username/password from the OS keychain is used directly in the EAP authentication exchange; no browser is opened.

If MFAEnabled: true but a valid DSID cookie is already cached in the session parameters (from a previous vpnconnect mfa run), the browser is not opened again.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• In automated mode, only Microsoft Azure AD / Office 365 SSO flows are supported. Other SAML or OIDC identity providers are not tested.
  • The CSS/XPath selectors used in automated mode are hard-coded and may break if Microsoft changes the sign-in page layout. Manual mode has no such limitation.
  • The browser runs on the desktop; headless mode is not used because MFA challenges require user interaction.

docs.title: PAC file parsing
docs.text: This document explains how VPNConnect processes Proxy Auto-Config (PAC) files received from Pulse Secure VPN servers and why this is necessary for correct DNS and split-tunnel behaviour.

docs.title: What is a PAC file in this context?
docs.text:
Pulse Secure VPN servers sometimes deliver a PAC (Proxy Auto-Config) file to clients as part of the network configuration exchange. A PAC file contains a JavaScript function — FindProxyForURL — that, for each destination, returns either "PROXY <address>" (route via a proxy) or "DIRECT" (connect directly).

For enterprise networks the PAC file encodes the topology of the internal network: which domains and addresses are internal (and therefore reachable only through the VPN) and which are public (and should go directly to the internet).

VPNConnect does not run the JavaScript. Instead, it statically parses the PAC source to extract domain patterns, which are then used to configure DNS search domains on the VPN interface.

docs.title: Why extract DNS search domains from PAC?
docs.text:
Some Pulse Secure deployments do not send explicit DNS search domains (AVP attribute 0x4006). Their PAC file is the only place where the list of internal domains is expressed. If vpnconnect did not parse the PAC file, DNS queries for internal resources would not be routed through the VPN's DNS servers.

By extracting PROXY target domains from the PAC file and adding them as DNS search domains, vpnconnect ensures that names like internal.example.com resolve via the VPN DNS servers rather than the upstream public resolver.

docs.title: How the parser works
docs.text:
The PACParser in internal/pulse/pac uses regular expressions to find conditional blocks in the PAC JavaScript and identify their associated actions.

Extraction steps:

  1. Find if/else if blocks containing return "PROXY ..." or return "DIRECT".
  2. Inside those blocks, look for calls to domain-matching functions:
  3. dnsDomainIs(host, ".example.com")
  4. shExpMatch(host, "*.internal.corp")
  5. Extract the domain patterns from the function arguments.
  6. Classify each pattern as a proxy route (internal) or direct route (public).

docs.title: Domain compression
docs.text:
Raw PAC files from enterprise deployments often contain hundreds of entries that are redundant when expressed as DNS search domains. The parser applies a compression step to reduce noise:

  1. Group by suffix: count how many distinct sub-domain patterns share a common suffix (e.g. a.example.com, b.example.com, c.example.com all share .example.com).
  2. Threshold promotion: if the count of sub-domains for a suffix exceeds a threshold, replace all of them with the root suffix (example.com).
  3. Remove dominated entries: once a broader pattern is present (e.g. example.com), remove any specific entries that are already covered (a.example.com is covered by example.com).

The result is a minimal, non-redundant set of domain suffixes that accurately represents the internal network scope.

docs.title: Integration with DNS configuration
docs.text:
When the Pulse client receives a PAC payload (attribute 0x4009) or a PAC URL (attribute 0x4023):

  1. A PACParser is instantiated with the PAC content.
  2. TransformPAC() is called to run the extraction and compression.
  3. GetDirectRoutes() returns the internal domain list (the DIRECT routes in the PAC, which correspond to publicly accessible names; the PROXY routes correspond to the VPN-internal names).
  4. The resulting domains are prepended to the DNS search domain list configured on the TUN interface.

Platform-specific DNS configuration (systemd-resolved on Linux, DNSZones on macOS, winipcfg on Windows) then ensures these domains are resolved via the VPN DNS servers.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• The parser only handles static domain patterns. Dynamic JavaScript logic (function calls, variable interpolation) is not evaluated.
  • Sufficient for most enterprise Pulse Secure deployments, which use straightforward domain lists.
  • The extraction is best-effort: unusual PAC patterns that deviate from the common dnsDomainIs / shExpMatch idioms may not be detected.

docs.title: Pulse protocol internals
docs.text: This document explains how VPNConnect implements the Juniper Pulse Secure VPN protocol.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The internal/pulse package provides a client-side implementation of the Pulse Secure VPN protocol. It covers:

  • IF-T/TLS authentication (username/password or DSID cookie)
  • EAP authentication (Identity, GTC, Juniper expanded types)
  • Network configuration parsing and application (IPv4, DNS, MTU, routes, PAC)
  • TUN/TAP interface creation and management
  • TLS-only data channel (fully implemented and production-ready)
  • ESP/DTLS data channel (implemented; not fully validated in all environments)
  • Platform-specific routing for endpoint exceptions (Linux, macOS, Windows)
  • Live connection status and statistics

Feature completeness

Feature Status TLS-only data channel Production-ready ESP/DTLS data channel Implemented; not guaranteed in all environments IPv4 Fully supported IPv6 Not supported Host checker / posture Not supported Advanced Pulse features Not supported

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Authentication uses the IF-T/TLS protocol (IF-T over TLS). The auth.Authenticator handles the full handshake:

  1. Open a TLS connection to the VPN endpoint.
  2. Send an HTTP Upgrade request to switch the connection to IF-T/TLS.
  3. Negotiate the IF-T protocol version.
  4. Send client information (hostname, local IP address).
  5. Perform EAP authentication:
  6. Identity: client announces its identity.
  7. GTC: generic token / password challenge.
  8. Juniper expanded: Juniper-specific EAP sub-types for session and cookie handling.
  9. Handle DSID cookie shortcuts: if the server sends a configuration packet early (because a valid DSID cookie was presented), the EAP flow is skipped.

Two authentication modes are supported:

  • Username/password: credentials are read from the profile (or OS keychain) and sent in the EAP-GTC response.
  • DSID cookie: a pre-obtained session cookie (e.g. from the MFA flow) is presented directly. This is the faster path for MFA-enabled profiles.

docs.title: Network configuration
docs.text:
After authentication succeeds the server sends a series of AVP (Attribute-Value Pair) packets containing the client's network configuration. The connection phase ends when a packet of type 0x8f is received.

Parsed attributes include:

Attribute Description IPv4 address and netmask Assigned address for the TUN interface MTU Maximum Transmission Unit for the tunnel DNS servers Primary and secondary resolver IPs DNS search domains Suffixes appended to unqualified queries Include routes Subnets to route through the VPN (split tunnelling) Exclude routes Subnets to bypass the VPN PAC file / PAC URL Proxy auto-config for extracting internal domains

For the full list of attributes and their support status, see Pulse protocol attributes.

PAC file processing extracts internal domain names from the PAC JavaScript and adds them as DNS search domains. See PAC file parsing for details.

docs.title: Data channels
docs.text: VPNConnect supports two data channel modes:

docs.title: TLS-only mode
docs.text:
Packets are encapsulated inside the existing IF-T/TLS connection. The tls.Handler reads IP packets from the TUN interface and writes them as IF-T frames to the TLS socket, and vice versa.

This mode is always available and is used as a fallback when ESP is unavailable or not negotiated.

docs.title: ESP / DTLS mode
docs.text:
A parallel UDP tunnel is established using ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload). The esp package handles key derivation, packet encryption (AES-256 by default), integrity protection (SHA-1 HMAC), and anti-replay sequence numbering.

ESP offers better throughput and lower latency than TLS because it avoids TCP head-of-line blocking. However, it requires UDP connectivity to the server on port 4500 (or the port specified by attribute 0x4016).

If the ESP handshake fails or times out (attribute 0x4017 controls the timeout), vpnconnect falls back to TLS-only mode automatically.

docs.title: Routing design
docs.text:
Two host routes are added to the system routing table before the TUN interface is brought up:

  1. VPN server IP → default gateway: ensures that control-plane traffic (TLS connection) is not routed into the VPN tunnel, which would cause a routing loop.
  2. ESP server IP → default gateway (if different from the TLS server): same reason, for the UDP data channel.

These routes are removed on disconnect.

All other traffic routing is determined by the include/exclude routes from the server configuration. VPNConnect applies these as direct ip route (Linux), route add (macOS), or RouteData (Windows) entries on the TUN/TAP interface.

docs.title: Key types
docs.text: Type Description Protocol Interface for VPN protocol implementations Client Main struct; implements Protocol ConnectionConfig All connection parameters and per-connection options ConnectionStatus Reports current state (connecting, connected, disconnected, error) auth.Authenticator Handles IF-T/TLS and EAP authentication tls.Handler Forwards packets between TUN and TLS socket esp.Tunnel Manages the ESP UDP data channel

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• IPv6 only for routing: IPv6 is supported for split-include/exclude routes and DNS, but the TUN interface itself is IPv4-only. Cross-protocol tunnelling (IPv6 inside an IPv4 ESP tunnel) depends on "Unstupid mode" support from the server (0x4024).
  • No host checker: Pulse Secure's host-checker / posture-assessment feature is not implemented.
  • ESP not production-validated: The ESP path is functionally complete but has not been tested in all real-world server configurations.

docs.title: How to build VPNConnect from source
docs.text: This guide shows how to compile VPNConnect on Linux, macOS, and Windows. Building from source is intended for developers and contributors.

docs.title: All platforms
docs.text:
• Go 1.25.5+ — Download
  • Node.js 18+ and npm — required to build the Wails frontend
  • Task (go-task) — Install
  • goreleaser v2 — Install
  • CGO enabled — the build requires C compiler toolchain

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
# Debian/Ubuntu
sudo apt install build-essential libgtk-3-dev libwebkit2gtk-4.1-dev

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
• Xcode Command Line Tools: xcode-select --install
  • Minimum deployment target: macOS 14.0
  • Required link flags: -framework UniformTypeIdentifiers

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
• MSYS2 / MinGW-w64 (for CGO)
  • The Wintun driver DLL is downloaded automatically by the build; no manual setup needed.

docs.title: Clone the repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/VPNConnectDev/vpnconnect.git
cd vpnconnect

docs.title: Build for the current platform
docs.text:
task build-single-target

This runs goreleaser build --snapshot --clean --single-target, which:

  1. Runs go mod tidy.
  2. Installs frontend npm dependencies (npm install in cmd/vpnconnect/ui/frontend).
  3. Builds the Vite frontend (npm run build).
  4. Compiles the Go binary with the desktop and production build tags.

The resulting binary is placed in the dist/ directory.

docs.title: Build a release package
docs.text:
task release-single-target

Runs goreleaser release --snapshot --clean --single-target. In addition to the binary, this produces the platform-specific installer package (.deb, .rpm, .pkg, .exe installer) in dist/.

docs.title: Direct Go build (advanced)
docs.text:
If you want to avoid goreleaser and build directly:

# Build the frontend first
cd cmd/vpnconnect/ui/frontend
npm install
npm run build
cd ../../../..

# Build the binary
go build -tags=desktop,production -o bin/vpnconnect .

docs.title: Linux-specific flags
docs.text: No additional flags required beyond CGO.

docs.title: macOS-specific flags
docs.text:
CGO_CFLAGS="-mmacosx-version-min=14.0" \
CGO_LDFLAGS="-mmacosx-version-min=14.0 -framework UniformTypeIdentifiers" \
go build -tags=desktop,production -o bin/vpnconnect .

docs.title: Windows-specific flags (GUI build)
docs.text: go build -tags=desktop,production -ldflags="-H windowsgui" -o bin/vpnconnect.exe .

docs.title: Cross-compilation
docs.text:
Cross-compilation requires Docker and the cross-compilation image:

task setup:docker

This builds the Docker image (~800 MB download) with the required toolchains for Linux, macOS, and Windows. After setup:

docker run --rm -v "$PWD":/src vpnconnect-cross goreleaser build --snapshot --clean

docs.title: Running tests
docs.text:
go test ./internal/...

Some tests require root privileges (TUN interface creation). Platform-specific test files use build tags (e.g. example_darwin_test.go).

docs.title: How to connect in standalone mode (no daemon)
docs.text: This guide shows how to use vpnconnect connect --standalone to establish a VPN connection without a running daemon. The connection is managed entirely in the current process.

docs.title: When to use standalone mode
docs.text:
Standalone mode is useful when:

  • You are on a system where installing a system service is not practical (e.g. a CI runner, a container, or a temporary machine).
  • You want to test a connection interactively without setting up the full daemon.
  • You need programmatic control: the process exits when the VPN is no longer needed.

Trade-offs compared to daemon mode:

Daemon mode Standalone mode Requires root for connect No (daemon already has it) Yes Stays connected after terminal closes Yes No (process exits) status / statistics commands work Yes No UI integration Yes No

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• The vpnconnect binary installed.
  • A profile created. See How to manage VPN profiles.
  • Root / administrator privileges in the current shell.

docs.title: Connect in standalone mode
docs.text:
sudo vpnconnect connect --profile work --standalone

The process blocks, printing status every 30 seconds:

Starting VPN connection in standalone mode...
VPN connection established in standalone mode.

Running in daemon mode (press Ctrl+C to stop)...
[10:15:30] Interface utun5 still active
[10:16:00] Interface utun5 still active

Press Ctrl+C or send SIGTERM to disconnect cleanly:

Received signal interrupt, cleaning up...

docs.title: With MFA
docs.text:
If the profile has --mfa enabled, the browser window opens before the connection. Complete the MFA prompt, then the connection proceeds as normal and the process blocks.

sudo vpnconnect connect --profile work-mfa --standalone

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
Increase verbosity to trace connection issues:

sudo vpnconnect --log-level=debug connect --profile work --standalone

Logs go to stderr. Pipe or redirect as needed:

sudo vpnconnect --log-level=debug connect --profile work --standalone 2>vpn.log

docs.title: How to connect with MFA
docs.text:
This guide shows how to set up and use a VPN profile that uses browser-based multi-factor authentication. It covers both manual mode (any identity provider) and automated mode (Microsoft Azure AD / Office 365).

For a conceptual explanation of the two modes, see MFA browser automation.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• VPNConnect installed and the daemon running. See How to install VPNConnect.
  • A Pulse Secure VPN endpoint that redirects to a browser-based MFA login.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create an MFA-enabled profile
docs.text:
vpnconnect profile add \
  --name work \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice@example.com \
  --mfa \
  --store-password=false

The --mfa flag enables the browser-based authentication flow. By default the browser opens at the VPN URL and waits for you to log in manually — this works with any identity provider.

To have VPNConnect fill in your Microsoft SSO username and password automatically, add --mfa-automate:

vpnconnect profile add \
  --name work \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice@example.com \
  --mfa --mfa-automate \
  --store-password=false

With --mfa-automate, the browser enters your credentials up to the MFA challenge, then waits for you to approve the push notification or enter a TOTP code. This mode requires a user@domain.com format username and is tailored to the Microsoft Azure AD login page.

docs.title: Step 2 — Connect
docs.text:
vpnconnect connect --profile work

A Chrome browser window opens and navigates to the VPN endpoint. Complete any login steps and the MFA challenge. The window closes automatically once the DSID cookie is obtained.

The connection is then handed to the daemon and the connect command returns.

To override the automation setting for a single invocation without editing the profile:

vpnconnect connect --profile work --mfa-automate

docs.title: Pre-authenticating (optional)
docs.text:
If you want to obtain the DSID cookie ahead of time — for example, to time-box the browser interaction separately from the connection — use the mfa command:

vpnconnect mfa --profile work

Then connect immediately after:

vpnconnect connect --profile work

Because a valid DSID cookie is now cached, connect will not open a browser.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Problem Likely cause Resolution Browser opens but automated login fails immediately Wrong username format (automated mode) Use user@domain.com "MFA failed" error after browser closes DSID cookie not found The post-auth proceed button may have changed selector; check logs with --log-level=debug Browser times out (5-minute limit) MFA prompt not completed in time Re-run connect and complete the MFA faster "Maximum sessions reached" dialog Orphaned session on the server Click through the dialog to replace the old session; vpnconnect handles this automatically Automated mode fails at password field Microsoft page layout changed Remove --mfa-automate and complete the form manually, or check logs for selector errors

docs.title: How to install VPNConnect
docs.text:
This guide shows how to install VPNConnect as a system service (daemon) on Linux, macOS, and Windows. The service runs as root/SYSTEM and manages your VPN connections; you interact with it through the CLI or the desktop UI without needing elevated privileges yourself.

If you want to run vpnconnect without installing a daemon, see How to connect in standalone mode.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text: Pre-built packages are available for common distributions. Download the appropriate package from the Releases page.

docs.title: Debian / Ubuntu (.deb)
docs.text: sudo dpkg -i ~/Downloads/vpnconnect_<version>_amd64.deb

docs.title: RHEL / Fedora (.rpm)
docs.text: sudo dnf install ~/Downloads/vpnconnect-<version>.x86_64.rpm

docs.title: Arch (.pkg.tar.zst)
docs.text: sudo pacman -U ~/Downloads/vpnconnect-<version>-x86_64.pkg.tar.zst

docs.title: Alpine (.apk)
docs.text:
sudo apk add --allow-untrusted ~/Downloads/vpnconnect_<version>_x86_64.apk

After installation the vpnconnect-server systemd service is enabled and started automatically. You can manage it with:

sudo systemctl status vpnconnect-server
sudo systemctl start vpnconnect-server
sudo systemctl stop vpnconnect-server

Logs are written to the systemd journal:

journalctl -u vpnconnect-server -f

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
1. Download vpnconnect-macos-installer.pkg from the Releases page.
  2. Open the .pkg file and follow the installer prompts. Administrator rights are required.
  3. The VPNConnect daemon is registered as a launchd service and starts automatically.

Manage the daemon manually with:

sudo launchctl load   /Library/LaunchDaemons/org.vpnconnect.server.plist
sudo launchctl unload /Library/LaunchDaemons/org.vpnconnect.server.plist

Daemon logs: /var/log/vpnconnect-server.log

Note: macOS Gatekeeper will block the first launch of the UI app because it is not signed and notarized. Go to System Settings → Privacy & Security and click Open Anyway to authorise it.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
1. Download vpnconnect-windows-installer.exe from the Releases page.
  2. Run the installer. Administrator rights are required.
  3. The VPNConnect service is installed and started automatically. It is visible in the Services console (services.msc).
  4. Launch the UI from the Start menu by searching for VPNConnect.

Manage the service from an elevated command prompt:

sc start VPNConnect
sc stop VPNConnect
sc query VPNConnect

docs.title: How to manage VPN profiles
docs.text:
This guide shows how to create, list, edit, export, import, and remove VPN profiles. A profile stores the endpoint URL, username, and connection options; the password is kept separately in the OS keychain.

For a full description of all flags, see the CLI reference.

docs.title: Create a profile
docs.text:
vpnconnect profile add \
  --name work \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice

You will be prompted for a password; it is stored in the OS keychain. To skip password storage:

vpnconnect profile add \
  --name work \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice \
  --store-password=false

docs.title: Create a profile with MFA enabled
docs.text:
For endpoints that use browser-based MFA (any identity provider):

vpnconnect profile add \
  --name work-mfa \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice@example.com \
  --mfa \
  --store-password=false

To also automate Microsoft Azure AD / Office 365 credential entry, add --mfa-automate:

vpnconnect profile add \
  --name work-mfa \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice@example.com \
  --mfa --mfa-automate \
  --store-password=false

See How to connect with MFA for details on both modes.

docs.title: List profiles
docs.text:
vpnconnect profile list

Output:

NAME       ENDPOINT                   USERNAME  MFA  MERGE_DNS  CREATED
----       --------                   --------  ---  ---------  -------
work       https://vpn.example.com    alice     No   No         2026-01-15
work-mfa   https://vpn.example.com    alice     Yes  No         2026-03-01

docs.title: Update the stored password
docs.text:
vpnconnect profile set-password --name work

You will be prompted for the new password. To supply it non-interactively (e.g. in a script):

vpnconnect profile set-password --name work --password "new-secret"

docs.title: Rename a profile
docs.text: vpnconnect profile rename --old-name work --new-name work-vpn

docs.title: Remove a profile
docs.text:
vpnconnect profile remove --name work

You will be asked to confirm. The profile file and its keychain entry are both deleted.

docs.title: Export and import profiles
docs.text: Exporting saves profile configuration to a JSON file so you can back it up or move it to another machine. Passwords are never included in exports — you will need to re-enter them after importing.

docs.title: Export from the UI
docs.text:
• Single profile: open the profile in the editor and click Export in the action bar. A <name>.json file is downloaded.
  • All profiles: click the download icon in the Profiles sidebar header. A vpnconnect-profiles.json file is downloaded containing all profiles as a JSON array.

docs.title: Import from the UI
docs.text:
Click the upload icon in the Profiles sidebar header and select a .json file. The file may contain a single profile object or an array of profiles. If an imported profile name conflicts with an existing one, it is renamed to <name> (imported) automatically. After import, set the password for each new profile via the profile editor or:

vpnconnect profile set-password --name "work (imported)"

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Profile files are stored at a platform-specific location:
  • Linux / macOS: ~/.config/vpnconnect/profiles/
  • Windows: %APPDATA%\vpnconnect\profiles\
  • Passwords are stored using the native OS keychain (libsecret on Linux, Keychain on macOS, Windows Credential Manager).
  • The maximum number of profiles is capped. Run vpnconnect profile list to see the current count against the limit.

docs.title: How to uninstall VPNConnect
docs.text: This guide shows how to completely remove VPNConnect from your system.

docs.title: Debian / Ubuntu (.deb)
docs.text: sudo dpkg -r vpnconnect

docs.title: RHEL / Fedora (.rpm)
docs.text: sudo dnf remove vpnconnect

docs.title: Arch (.pkg.tar.zst)
docs.text: sudo pacman -R vpnconnect

docs.title: Alpine (.apk)
docs.text: sudo apk del vpnconnect

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
Run the bundled uninstaller to remove the application, CLI binary, launchd service, and log file:

sudo /Applications/vpnconnect-uninstall.command

Alternatively, double-click vpnconnect-uninstall.command in /Applications.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
Uninstall VPNConnect through Add or Remove Programs (Settings → Apps), or run the uninstaller directly from the installation folder (usually C:\Program Files\VPNConnect\uninstall.exe).

The uninstaller stops and removes the Windows service, removes the CLI and UI binaries, and cleans up registry entries.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text:
Complete reference for all vpnconnect commands, subcommands, and flags.

Tip: Run vpnconnect <command> --help at any time to see inline help for that command.

docs.title: Global flags
docs.text:
These flags are available on every command.

Flag Default Description --log-level warn Log verbosity. One of: trace, debug, info, warn, error

docs.title: vpnconnect
docs.text:
vpnconnect [command] [flags]

VPN client with TUN/TAP interface management and Pulse protocol support. If no command is given, the graphical UI is launched (equivalent to vpnconnect ui).

docs.title: connect
docs.text:
Establish a VPN connection to a Juniper Pulse endpoint using a saved profile.

vpnconnect connect [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Default Description --profile "" Name of the profile to use. If omitted, the default profile is used --standalone false Run without the daemon. The connection is managed entirely in the current process. Requires root/administrator privileges --prompt-password false Prompt for a password even when one is stored in the OS keychain --merge-dns false Merge the profile's DNS list with the server-provided DNS list instead of replacing it --mfa-automate false Automate Microsoft SSO credential entry during MFA. Overrides the profile's mfa_automate setting for this invocation

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• In normal (non-standalone) mode connect sends the connection request to the running daemon and returns immediately. The daemon manages the interface lifecycle.
  • In --standalone mode the process blocks until interrupted (Ctrl+C / SIGTERM). See How to connect in standalone mode.
  • If the profile has MFAEnabled: true and no cached DSID cookie is available, the MFA browser flow is triggered automatically before sending the connection request. By default the browser waits for manual login; pass --mfa-automate to have credentials filled automatically (Microsoft SSO only).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Connect using the profile named "work"
vpnconnect connect --profile work

# Connect without a daemon, running in the foreground
sudo vpnconnect connect --profile work --standalone

# Always prompt for a password (ignores stored keychain entry)
vpnconnect connect --profile work --prompt-password

# Enable Microsoft SSO automation for this connection only
vpnconnect connect --profile work --mfa-automate

docs.title: disconnect
docs.text:
Disconnect from an active Pulse VPN connection and restore network settings.

vpnconnect disconnect [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Default Description --profile "" Profile name (optional). When omitted, the active connection is disconnected

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
vpnconnect disconnect
vpnconnect disconnect --profile work

docs.title: status
docs.text:
Query the daemon for the current VPN connection state and details.

vpnconnect status

No flags. Prints the current connection state and per-connection details (endpoint, interface, IP address, etc.) reported by the daemon.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: vpnconnect status

docs.title: statistics
docs.text:
Query the daemon for live packet and byte counters for the active VPN connection.

vpnconnect statistics

No flags. Prints transmitted and received packet/byte counts.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: vpnconnect statistics

docs.title: mfa
docs.text:
Perform only the MFA (browser) authentication step for a profile and cache the resulting DSID cookie. Useful for pre-authenticating before a connect without triggering the browser popup at connect time.

vpnconnect mfa --profile <name>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Required Default Description --profile Yes — Profile name to authenticate --mfa-automate No false Automate Microsoft SSO credential entry. Overrides the profile's mfa_automate setting for this invocation

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
vpnconnect mfa --profile work

# Enable Microsoft SSO automation for this pre-authentication only
vpnconnect mfa --profile work --mfa-automate

docs.title: profile
docs.text:
Manage VPN profiles. Profiles store the endpoint URL, username, and connection options. Passwords are stored separately in the OS keychain.

vpnconnect profile <subcommand> [flags]

docs.title: Subcommands
docs.text: Subcommand Description add Create a new profile list List all saved profiles remove Delete a profile rename Rename a profile set-password Set or update the keychain password for a profile

docs.title: profile add
docs.text:
Create a new Pulse VPN profile.

vpnconnect profile add --name <name> --endpoint <url> --username <user> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Required Default Description --name Yes — Unique profile name --endpoint Yes — VPN endpoint URL (e.g. https://vpn.example.com) --username Yes — Username for authentication --password No "" Password. If omitted and --store-password is true, you will be prompted --mfa No false Enable browser-based MFA for this profile --mfa-automate No false Automate Microsoft SSO credential entry during MFA. Requires --mfa. Tailored to the Microsoft Azure AD login page --store-password No true Store the password in the OS keychain --merge-dns No false Merge profile DNS with server DNS instead of replacing

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Basic profile, password stored in keychain
vpnconnect profile add --name work --endpoint https://vpn.example.com --username alice

# Profile with MFA (manual mode — works with any identity provider)
vpnconnect profile add --name work-mfa \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice \
  --mfa \
  --store-password=false

# Profile with MFA and automated Microsoft SSO credential entry
vpnconnect profile add --name work-mfa \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice@example.com \
  --mfa --mfa-automate \
  --store-password=false

docs.title: profile list
docs.text:
Display all saved profiles.

vpnconnect profile list

No flags. Prints a table with name, endpoint, username, MFA status, merge-DNS setting, and creation date.

docs.title: profile remove
docs.text:
Delete a profile and its associated credentials.

vpnconnect profile remove --name <name>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text:
Flag Required Description --name Yes Name of the profile to delete

You will be asked to confirm before deletion.

docs.title: profile rename
docs.text:
Rename a profile.

vpnconnect profile rename --old-name <name> --new-name <name>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Required Description --old-name Yes Current profile name --new-name Yes New profile name

docs.title: profile set-password
docs.text:
Set or update the password stored in the OS keychain for a profile.

vpnconnect profile set-password --name <name> [--password <password>]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Required Description --name Yes Profile name --password No New password. If omitted, you will be prompted

docs.title: server
docs.text:
Start the VPNConnect background daemon. The daemon listens on a random local TCP port and manages the VPN interface lifecycle on behalf of connect, disconnect, and other client commands.

sudo vpnconnect server

No flags. Requires root / administrator privileges. Intended to be run as a system service (systemd, launchd, Windows Service). See How to install VPNConnect for service configuration.

The daemon writes a server info file (port + auth token) at a platform-specific path so that client commands can locate it:

Platform Path Linux /run/vpnconnect/server.json macOS /var/run/vpnconnect/server.json Windows %ProgramData%\vpnconnect\server.json

docs.title: tuntap
docs.text:
Manage TUN/TAP network interfaces directly, bypassing the daemon. Useful for debugging or scripted environments.

vpnconnect tuntap <subcommand> [flags]

docs.title: Subcommands
docs.text: Subcommand Description create Create and configure a TUN/TAP interface remove Remove an existing interface list List all TUN/TAP interfaces on the system

docs.title: tuntap create
docs.text:
Create and configure a new TUN/TAP interface. Requires root/administrator privileges.

vpnconnect tuntap create --name <name> --type <tun|tap> --ip <addr> --netmask <mask> [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Required Default Description --name Yes — Interface name (e.g. vpn0, tun1) --type Yes — Interface type: tun or tap --ip Yes — IP address to assign --netmask Yes — Netmask (e.g. 255.255.255.0) --mtu No 0 MTU for the interface. 0 uses the system default --routes No — Additional routes to add (repeatable, CIDR notation) --dns No — DNS servers to configure (repeatable) --up-script No "" Script to run after the interface is up --down-script No "" Script to run before the interface is removed --persist No false Keep the interface after the process exits --daemon No false Block and keep the interface up until interrupted

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
sudo vpnconnect tuntap create \
  --name vpn0 \
  --type tun \
  --ip 10.0.0.2 \
  --netmask 255.255.255.0 \
  --mtu 1400 \
  --daemon

docs.title: tuntap remove
docs.text:
Remove an existing TUN/TAP interface.

vpnconnect tuntap remove --name <name>

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Required Description --name Yes Interface name to remove

docs.title: tuntap list
docs.text:
List all TUN/TAP interfaces currently present on the system.

vpnconnect tuntap list

No flags.

docs.title: ui
docs.text:
Launch the graphical user interface. This is the default command when vpnconnect is invoked with no arguments.

vpnconnect ui

No flags. Opens the Wails-based desktop application. Only one instance is permitted (enforced via UniqueID: com.vpnconnect.ui); a second invocation will focus the existing window.

docs.title: version
docs.text:
Print build version information.

vpnconnect version

No flags. Prints the version string, git commit hash, and build date.

docs.title: Example output
docs.text:
vpnconnect version: 1.0.0
commit: abc1234
build date: 2026-04-10

docs.title: Pulse Protocol Attributes
docs.text: This reference describes the Pulse (Juniper) VPN configuration attributes sent by the server during the configuration phase of a connection. It lists which attributes are supported, ignored, or unknown in vpnconnect.

docs.title: Attribute table
docs.text: Attribute Meaning Status Notes 0x0001 Internal IPv4 Address Supported Legacy IPv4 assignment 0x0002 Internal IPv4 Netmask Supported Netmask for internal IP 0x0003 IPv4 DNS Server Supported Primary / secondary DNS 0x0004 IPv4 WINS Server Supported NetBIOS name servers 0x0008 Internal IPv6 Address Supported IPv6 assignment (128-bit + prefix) 0x000a IPv6 DNS Server Supported 0x000f IPv6 Split-Include Supported IPv6 routes to tunnel 0x0010 IPv6 Split-Exclude Supported IPv6 routes to bypass 0x0014 Prefer FQDN resources Ignored Priority of FQDN over IP in conflicts 0x4000 Route Change Policy Ignored Disconnect if local routes change 0x4001 Tunnel Precedence Ignored Tunnel routes take precedence over LAN 0x4005 Tunnel MTU Supported Maximum Transmission Unit 0x4006 DNS Search Domains Supported Search suffixes for DNS 0x4007 Unknown (Integer) Ignored Value 0x01 observed; logged 0x4008 Proxy String Ignored Static proxy configuration 0x4009 PAC Payload Supported Auto-config script content 0x400a Preserve Proxy Ignored Keep existing local proxy settings 0x400b IPv4 Internal Gateway Supported Meaningless for L3 tunnel (ignored by OS) 0x400c Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown 0x400d Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown 0x400e Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown 0x400f Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown 0x4010 ESP Encryption Supported Algorithm ID (e.g. 0x05 = AES-256) 0x4011 ESP HMAC Supported MAC algorithm (e.g. 0x02 = SHA-1) 0x4012 ESP Lifetime (seconds) Supported Time before ESP rekey 0x4013 ESP Lifetime (bytes) Supported Data volume before ESP rekey 0x4014 ESP Anti-Replay Supported Enable / disable sequence-number check 0x4015 TOS Copy Ignored Copy TOS bits from inner to outer packet 0x4016 ESP UDP Port Supported Port for ESP encapsulation (usually 4500) 0x4017 ESP Fallback Timer Supported Seconds before falling back to TLS data channel 0x4018 Unknown (Timer?) Ignored Value 60 observed; logged 0x4019 Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown 0x401a ESP Only Mode Supported If 1, do not attempt TLS data channel 0x401b Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown 0x401c Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown 0x401e IPv6 Internal Gateway Supported Meaningless for L3 tunnel 0x401f Subnet Access Policy Ignored Access to local subnets when connected 0x4020 Enforce IPv4 Ignored Server forces IPv4 usage 0x4021 Enforce IPv6 Ignored Server forces IPv6 usage 0x4022 Disable Proxy Ignored Force-disable any client proxy 0x4023 PAC URL Supported Remote location for PAC script 0x4024 Pulse "Unstupid" Mode Ignored Allows cross-protocol tunnelling (6in4 / 4in6) 0x4025 9.1R14 version marker Ignored Informational server version advertisement 0x4026 9.1R16 version marker Ignored Informational server version advertisement 0x4027 Unknown Ignored Logged as unknown

docs.title: Network layer configuration (0x0001–0x0010)
docs.text: Define the core IP configuration for the VPN tunnel. vpnconnect supports both IPv4 and IPv6 assignment, DNS servers, and split-tunnelling routes.

docs.title: ESP security parameters (0x4010–0x4017)
docs.text: Define the parallel ESP (UDP) tunnel used for higher throughput. They specify encryption and integrity algorithms, rekeying intervals, and anti-replay protection. vpnconnect uses these to negotiate the ESP session; see Pulse protocol internals for details.

docs.title: Pulse ESP "Unstupid" Mode (0x4024)
docs.text: Normally Pulse requires IPv6 traffic over IPv6 transport and IPv4 over IPv4. When set to 1 this attribute indicates the server supports cross-protocol tunnelling (e.g. sending IPv6 packets over an IPv4 ESP tunnel). If 0 and a cross-protocol packet must be sent, vpnconnect falls back to the IF-T/TLS data channel.

docs.title: Routing and proxy policies (0x4000, 0x4001, 0x401f, 0x4022)
docs.text: Communicate the server's desired client-side policies. 0x4000/0x4001 prevent dual-homing or ensure the tunnel route is preferred. 0x4022 and related attributes control whether the client should use, ignore, or preserve system-wide proxy settings. These are currently ignored.

docs.title: Informational markers (0x4025, 0x4026)
docs.text: Sent by newer Pulse Secure / Ivanti Connect Secure servers to advertise version capabilities (e.g. 9.1R14+). They carry no configuration payload but are useful for debugging protocol version compatibility.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• openconnect source code (pulse.c)
  • Implementation: internal/pulse/config/parser.go

docs.title: Getting started: your first VPN connection
docs.text:
In this tutorial we will install VPNConnect, create a VPN profile, connect to the VPN, verify the connection is working, and then disconnect cleanly. By the end you will have made a complete round-trip and will know the core workflow.

We will use the CLI throughout. The UI app wraps the same operations in a graphical interface; once you understand the CLI, the UI will feel familiar.

docs.title: What you will need
docs.text:
• A computer running Linux, macOS, or Windows.
  • Administrator / root access to install the daemon.
  • The address of a Pulse Secure VPN endpoint, a username, and a password (provided by your organisation's IT department).

docs.title: Step 1 — Install VPNConnect
docs.text:
Download and install the package for your operating system from the Releases page.

Linux (Debian/Ubuntu):

sudo dpkg -i ~/Downloads/vpnconnect_<version>_amd64.deb

macOS:

Open the .pkg installer and follow the prompts.

Windows:

Run the .exe installer.

After installation the VPNConnect daemon starts automatically as a system service. You can confirm it is running:

# Linux
systemctl status vpnconnect-server

# macOS
sudo launchctl list | grep vpnconnect

# Windows (elevated prompt)
sc query VPNConnect

You should see the service in a running state. If it is not running, start it:

# Linux
sudo systemctl start vpnconnect-server

# macOS
sudo launchctl load /Library/LaunchDaemons/org.vpnconnect.server.plist

docs.title: Step 2 — Create a VPN profile
docs.text:
A profile stores the endpoint address, your username, and connection options. Run:

vpnconnect profile add \
  --name my-first-vpn \
  --endpoint https://vpn.example.com \
  --username alice

Replace https://vpn.example.com with the address of your VPN gateway and alice with your username.

You will be prompted for a password:

Enter password (leave empty to skip):
✓ Password stored securely in keychain
✓ Profile 'my-first-vpn' created successfully

Verify the profile was saved:

vpnconnect profile list

You should see your new profile in the output table.

docs.title: Step 3 — Connect
docs.text:
vpnconnect connect --profile my-first-vpn

vpnconnect loads the profile, retrieves the password from the keychain, and sends the connection request to the daemon. The daemon authenticates with the VPN server and brings up the TUN interface.

On success you will see:

✓ Connected via server

If your organisation uses MFA (Microsoft SSO): Add --mfa to the profile add command and see How to connect with MFA. A browser window will open the first time you connect; complete the MFA prompt and it will close automatically.

docs.title: Step 4 — Verify the connection
docs.text:
Check the connection status:

vpnconnect status

You should see output like:

Status: ok
Connection details:
  state: connected
  profile: my-first-vpn
  interface: tun0
  ip: 10.100.5.42
  endpoint: https://vpn.example.com

Notice the ip field — that is the address assigned to you on the VPN network. You can also check whether VPN-only resources are reachable (e.g. ping an internal server your IT team has told you about).

docs.title: Step 5 — View live statistics
docs.text:
While connected, observe how many packets have been exchanged:

vpnconnect statistics

Statistics status: ok
Packet statistics:
  {
    "rx_bytes": 12840,
    "rx_packets": 95,
    "tx_bytes": 9320,
    "tx_packets": 88
  }

Run it again in a few seconds. You should see the counters increase as background traffic flows through the tunnel.

docs.title: Step 6 — Disconnect
docs.text:
vpnconnect disconnect

✓ Disconnected from Pulse VPN (managed by server)

VPNConnect sends a proper logout packet to the server (releasing your session slot immediately), removes the VPN routes, and tears down the TUN interface. Your network returns to its normal state.

docs.title: What you have learned
docs.text:
• VPNConnect is split into a privileged daemon and unprivileged clients (CLI, UI).
  • Profiles store connection settings; passwords live in the OS keychain.
  • connect, status, statistics, and disconnect are the core daily commands.
  • The daemon manages the interface lifecycle; clients just send commands.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Multiple VPN endpoints? Add more profiles: vpnconnect profile add …
  • MFA required? Follow How to connect with MFA.
  • Want to explore all commands? Read the CLI reference.
  • Curious how it works? See Architecture: daemon, IPC, and connection lifecycle.
